sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Evaluation of a new right eye twitch.
History of Present Illness
Karen Nelson, a 3-year-old female, presents with a new right eye twitch noticed approximately two weeks ago. The patient's father reports the twitch varies in frequency, sometimes several times per hour, and other days it is not noticeable until late afternoon. Karen reports that the twitch does not cause pain, but she notices her face moving when it occurs. She enjoys playing soccer and reports that a girl named Isabella sometimes kicks her and pulls her hair. She also experiences occasional 'mcdoubles' after soccer, which she finds enjoyable with her father. She denies any prior seizures or family history of neurological disorders. She has been eating and playing well and has not experienced any other symptoms, including fever or headaches.
Findings
Physical exam reveals normal cranial nerves, reflexes are two plus and symmetric, and sensation is intact. Gait is steady. The patient demonstrates voluntary movements as directed. No focal neurologic deficits are noted. A video recording of the twitch was requested by the patient.
Assessment
The patient presents with a transient tic, commonly observed in children, particularly during school years. The twitch does not appear to be causing distress or impacting her daily activities.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: I recommend observing the tic and documenting its frequency and any associated triggers. We will not initiate any medication or treatment at this time. I will order a video recording of the tic to further evaluate the presentation. I will schedule a follow-up in four weeks to assess the tic's progression and discuss further management options if needed.
